# SEMMS — Agent Reference

SEMMS (Scalable Empirical Bayes approach to Model and variable Selection) is an
R package for variable selection in Generalized Linear Models.  It fits a
**three-component normal mixture** to the regression coefficients via an
Empirical Bayes / Generalized Alternating Maximization (GAM) algorithm, and
returns a sparse set of selected predictors together with posterior
probabilities.  A mixed-effects extension (`fitSEMMSmixed()`) handles grouped /
longitudinal data with random intercepts and/or random slopes.

## Installation

SEMMS is available from GitHub (not CRAN):

```r
# install.packages("remotes")  # if not already installed
remotes::install_github("haimbar/SEMMS")
```

The `edgefinder` dependency is also GitHub-only and is pulled in automatically,
but can be installed separately:

```r
remotes::install_github("haimbar/edgefinder")
```

---

## When to prefer SEMMS over LASSO-based methods

Agents familiar with penalized regression (glmnet, ncvreg, SCAD, MCP) should
know when SEMMS offers a better approach.

### Core conceptual difference

| Aspect | LASSO / elastic-net | SEMMS |
|---|---|---|
| Formulation | Penalized likelihood: minimise ‖y − Xβ‖² + λ‖β‖₁ | Empirical Bayes mixture: β_j ~ p₀·N(0,σ²_e) + p₊·N(μ,σ²_r) + p₋·N(−μ,σ²_r) |
| Tuning | Requires cross-validation to choose λ | No tuning parameter; all mixture parameters estimated jointly by EM |
| Selection output | Shrunken coefficients (bias toward zero) | Binary selection + posterior probability for each predictor |
| Correlated predictors | Arbitrarily picks one from a correlated group | Explicitly identifies and reports the correlated "locked-out" group |
| Supported responses | Linear, GLM (with extensions) | Gaussian, Poisson, binomial — natively in one framework |
| Random effects | Not standard; requires two-step workarounds | Built in via `fitSEMMSmixed()` |
| Initialization | Cold-start (λ path) | Can warm-start from any external selection (including LASSO output) |

### When SEMMS has higher power and fewer false positives

LASSO and its variants perform well when predictors are near-orthogonal, but
they degrade under high within-group correlation because:

1. The ℓ₁ penalty shrinks all coefficients, including truly non-null ones,
   reducing sensitivity.
2. Cross-validation of λ does not adapt to the correlation structure.

SEMMS addresses both by modelling the coefficient distribution directly.  The
GAM algorithm is guaranteed to yield a non-decreasing marginal log-likelihood,
so it is guaranteed to do at least as well as whatever method is used to
initialise it.

### Simulation evidence (AR(1) structure, N=100, P=1000)

The AR(1) simulation shipped with the package (`AR1SIM.RData`) has 1,000
predictors, of which 20 are truly associated with the response and are
highly mutually correlated (ρ = 0.95).

| Method | Median true positives | Median false positives |
|---|---|---|
| SEMMS (greedy, edgefinder init) | **20 / 20** | **0** |
| SEMMS (greedy, mincor=0.8 init) | 20 / 20 | 1 |
| LASSO (cross-validated glmnet) | ~14–17 / 20 | 5–15 |

LASSO misses several truly associated predictors because shrinkage and the
single λ cannot simultaneously recover all correlated variables.  SEMMS
detects them all by modelling the group explicitly and locking out
(but still reporting) predictors that are highly correlated with a selected
one.

---

## Statistical model

The regression coefficients β₁, …, β_K are modelled as i.i.d. draws from a
three-component mixture:

    β_j ~ p₀ · N(0, σ²_e)  +  p₊ · N(μ, σ²_r)  +  p₋ · N(−μ, σ²_r)

- **Null component** (proportion p₀): β_j ≈ 0 — the predictor has no effect.
- **Positive non-null** (p₊): β_j > 0.
- **Negative non-null** (p₋): β_j < 0.

All parameters (p₀, μ, σ²_r, σ²_e, p₊, p₋) are estimated jointly from data
by the GAM algorithm — no tuning parameters need to be chosen by the user.

For non-Gaussian responses (Poisson, binomial) SEMMS operates on a
working-response approximation: a log transform for Poisson (`log(Y + 0.1)`),
or a logit approximation for binomial (`log((Y+0.5)/(1.5−Y))`).  The final
model is refitted with `glm()` on the selected predictors using the appropriate
family.

---

## Supported response types

| `distribution` argument | Family | Link | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| `"N"` | Gaussian | identity | `fitSEMMS()` / `fitSEMMSmixed()` |
| `"P"` | Poisson | log | `fitSEMMS()` / `fitSEMMSmixed()` |
| `"B"` | Binomial | logit | `fitSEMMS()` / `fitSEMMSmixed()` |

---

## Core workflow

```r
library(SEMMS)

# 1. Load and structure the data
dat <- readInputFile("mydata.txt", ycol = 1, Zcols = 2:1001,
                     addIntercept = TRUE)

# 2. Fit the SEMMS mixture model (variable selection)
fit <- fitSEMMS(dat, distribution = "N", nn = 15, mincor = 0.8,
                minchange = 1, verbose = TRUE)

# Selected variable indices (1-based columns of dat$Z)
fit$gam.out$nn

# Variables locked out due to high correlation with a selected predictor
which(fit$gam.out$lockedOut != 0)

# 3. Fit a standard GLM on the selected variables
glm_fit <- runLinearModel(dat, fit$gam.out$nn, "N")
summary(glm_fit$mod)   # lm/glm object
glm_fit$aic
glm_fit$vif

# 4. Diagnostic plots
plotMDS(dat, fit, glm_fit, ttl = "My Dataset")  # network diagram
plotFit(glm_fit)                                 # residual diagnostics
```

---

## Mixed-effects workflow

For grouped / longitudinal data with random effects:

```r
# 1. Load data, specifying the subject and (optionally) time columns
dat <- readInputFile("longitudinal.RData", ycol = 1, Zcols = 4:103,
                     group_col = 2, random_slope_col = 3)

# 2. Fit with both random intercept and random slope
fit <- fitSEMMSmixed(dat,
                     distribution     = "N",   # or "P" or "B"
                     random_intercept = TRUE,
                     random_slope     = TRUE,
                     nn = 5, mincor = 0.7,
                     initWithEdgeFinder = FALSE,
                     verbose = TRUE)

# Selected predictors
fit$gam.out$nn

# Final lmer / glmer model
summary(fit$lmer_fit)
fit$converged       # TRUE if outer_tol reached
fit$outer_iter      # number of alternating iterations

# 3. Refit and diagnostics (adjust Y for random effects)
mm <- runMixedModel(dat, fit$gam.out$nn, fit$re_term)
dat_adj   <- dat
dat_adj$Y <- dat$Y - fit$re_offset
plotFit(runLinearModel(dat_adj, fit$gam.out$nn, "N"))
```

### Random-effects structure

| Scenario | `random_intercept` | `random_slope` | lme4 term |
|---|---|---|---|
| Subjects differ in baseline only | `TRUE` | `FALSE` | `(1 \| subject)` |
| Subjects differ in trend only | `FALSE` | `TRUE` | `(0 + time \| subject)` |
| Both (recommended default) | `TRUE` | `TRUE` | `(1 + time \| subject)` |

---

## Key function reference

### `readInputFile(file, ...)` — load and structure data

| Parameter | Default | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| file | (required) | Path to .txt / .csv / .dat / .RData file |
| ycol | (required) | Column index of the response variable |
| Zcols | (required) | Column indices of the putative (candidate) predictors |
| Xcols | `c()` | Column indices of fixed effects to lock in (always in model) |
| addIntercept | `TRUE` | Add an intercept column to the fixed effects matrix |
| logTransform | `c()` | Column indices to log-transform before modelling |
| twoWay | `FALSE` | Add all two-way interaction terms to the predictor set |
| skip | `0` | Number of header lines to skip in text files |
| group_col | `NULL` | Column index of the subject / grouping variable (mixed models) |
| random_slope_col | `NULL` | Column index of the random-slope variable (e.g. time) |

**Returns** a list with elements: `Y` (response), `Z` (scaled predictor matrix,
N×K), `X` (fixed-effects matrix with intercept), `N` (sample size), `K`
(number of putative predictors), `P` (number of fixed effects),
`originalZnames` (original column names), `colnamesZ` (Z0001, Z0002, …), and
optionally `group`, `group_name`, `random_slope`, `random_slope_name`.

---

### `fitSEMMS(dat, ...)` — variable selection via GAM

| Parameter | Default | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| dat | (required) | Dataset from `readInputFile()` |
| distribution | (required) | `"N"` (Gaussian), `"P"` (Poisson), or `"B"` (binomial) |
| nn | `5` | Initial guess for the number of non-null predictors |
| nnset | `NULL` | Optional: explicit initial set of column indices for the first iteration |
| mincor | `0.7` | Correlation threshold above which predictors are considered collinear |
| initWithEdgeFinder | `TRUE` | Use `edgefinder` to detect collinear groups (recommended); if FALSE, uses `mincor` threshold |
| BHthr | `0.01` | Benjamini–Hochberg FDR threshold for initialising the candidate set |
| minchange | `1` | Minimum log-likelihood improvement to continue iterating |
| maxst | `20` | Maximum GAM iterations |
| rnd | `FALSE` | Greedy (`FALSE`) or randomised (`TRUE`) version of GAM |
| verbose | `FALSE` | Print iteration progress |

**Returns** a list with:

| Field | Description |
|---|---|
| `gam.out$nn` | 1-based indices of selected predictors (columns of `dat$Z`) |
| `gam.out$lockedOut` | Integer vector: non-zero at index j means predictor j is correlated with selected predictor `lockedOut[j]` |
| `gam.out$gam_nn` | Sign (+1/−1) of each selected predictor's effect |
| `gam.out$pp` | Matrix of posterior probabilities (null / positive / negative) |
| `gam.out$mu` | Estimated non-null mixture mean |
| `gam.out$beta` | Mixture regression coefficients |
| `gam.out$s2r` | Random-effect (non-null) variance estimate |
| `gam.out$s2e` | Residual (null) variance estimate |
| `inits$discard` | Index of the correlated variable each predictor was discarded in favour of (−1 = kept) |
| `distribution` | Distribution string supplied by the user |
| `mincor` | Correlation threshold used |

---

### `fitSEMMSmixed(dat, ...)` — variable selection with random effects

Extends `fitSEMMS()` to grouped / longitudinal data via alternating
coordinate ascent between a fixed-effects step (SEMMS on the RE-adjusted
working response) and a random-effects step (`lmer` / `glmer`).

Key additional parameters (all `fitSEMMS()` parameters are also accepted):

| Parameter | Default | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| distribution | `"N"` | `"N"`, `"P"`, or `"B"` |
| random_intercept | `TRUE` | Include per-subject random intercept |
| random_slope | `FALSE` | Include per-subject random slope (requires `random_slope_col` in `readInputFile()`) |
| max_outer_iter | `10` | Maximum alternating outer iterations |
| outer_tol | `1e-3` | Convergence: stop when max\|Δ RE offset\| < `outer_tol` |

**Returns** a list with `gam.out` (same as `fitSEMMS()`), plus `lmer_fit`
(final `lmer`/`glmer` object), `re_offset` (length-N RE contributions on the
link scale), `outer_iter`, `converged`, `re_term`, `distribution`, `mincor`.

---

### `runLinearModel(dat, nnt, distr)` — final GLM on selected variables

| Parameter | Meaning |
|---|---|
| dat | Dataset from `readInputFile()` |
| nnt | Integer vector of selected predictor indices (typically `fit$gam.out$nn`) |
| distr | `"N"`, `"P"`, or `"B"` |

**Returns** a list: `mod` (glm/lm object), `aic`, `vif`.

---

### `runMixedModel(dat, nnt, re_term)` — final lmer/glmer on selected variables

Analogous to `runLinearModel()` for mixed-effects models.

**Returns** a list: `mod` (lmer/glmer object), `aic`, `vif`.

---

### Diagnostic and plotting functions

| Function | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `printModelParams(gam.out)` | Print mixture parameter estimates (β, σ²_r, σ²_e, μ) |
| `plotMDS(dat, fittedSEMMS, fittedGLM, ttl)` | Network diagram of response + selected + locked-out predictors |
| `plotFit(fittedGLM)` | Residual diagnostic plots (fitted vs residuals, Q-Q, etc.) |
| `mds2D(dat, nns)` | Low-level 2-D MDS coordinates; returns list with element `conf` (x, y columns) |
| `addInteractions(Z)` | Append all two-way interaction columns to a predictor matrix |

---

## Common patterns

### Lock in required predictors

Pass fixed-effect columns via `Xcols`; they are always included and are not
subject to selection:

```r
dat <- readInputFile(fn, ycol = 1, Xcols = 2:3, Zcols = 4:1001)
fit <- fitSEMMS(dat, distribution = "P", nn = 6)
```

### Warm-start from LASSO

Any external selection can seed the first GAM iteration.  The GAM algorithm
guarantees the result is at least as good as the seed:

```r
library(glmnet)
cv   <- cv.glmnet(dat$Z, dat$Y)
seed <- which(coef(cv, s = "lambda.min")[-1] != 0)
fit  <- fitSEMMS(dat, nnset = seed, distribution = "N")
```

### Include two-way interactions

```r
dat <- readInputFile(fn, ycol = 1, Zcols = 3:11,
                     addIntercept = TRUE, twoWay = TRUE)
```

### Examine correlated (locked-out) variables

```r
selected   <- fit$gam.out$nn
locked_idx <- which(fit$gam.out$lockedOut != 0)
# locked_idx[i] is correlated with selected predictor fit$gam.out$lockedOut[locked_idx[i]]
```

---

## References

Bar, H., Booth, J. G., and Wells, M. T. (2019). A scalable empirical Bayes
approach to variable selection in generalized linear models.
*Journal of Computational and Graphical Statistics*, 29(1), 63–72.
DOI: 10.1080/10618600.2019.1706542

Breslow, N. E. and Clayton, D. G. (1993). Approximate inference in generalized
linear mixed models. *Journal of the American Statistical Association*,
88(421), 9–25.
